title: Quiz: QEC on Trapped Ion Systems

questions.question: What is a key connectivity advantage of trapped ion systems for QEC?

options.text: Limited to nearest-neighbor connections
All-to-all connectivity within a trap zone
Only linear chains possible
Requires routing through intermediate ions

questions.explanation: Within a trap zone, any pair of ions can interact directly through their shared motional modes. This all-to-all connectivity allows implementation of any code structure without the routing overhead needed on fixed-connectivity hardware.

questions.question: Why might trapped ions favor the Steane code over the surface code?

options.text: Steane code has higher threshold
Steane code has transversal Clifford gates and fits naturally in all-to-all systems
Surface code cannot be implemented on ions
Steane code uses fewer physical qubits in total

questions.explanation: The Steane code has a transversal full Clifford group (H, S, CNOT) and fits well in systems with all-to-all connectivity. Surface code is optimized for 2D nearest-neighbor constraints that don't apply to trapped ions.

questions.question: What is the QCCD architecture?

options.text: A type of error correction code
A modular architecture using multiple trap zones connected by ion shuttling
A quantum gate design
A classical control system

questions.explanation: QCCD (Quantum Charge Coupled Device) is a scaling architecture where multiple trap zones are connected by junctions. Ions are shuttled between zones for operations, enabling scaling beyond the ~50-100 ion limit of single zones.

questions.question: How do trapped ion coherence times compare to superconducting qubits?

options.text: About the same (microseconds)
Trapped ions have much longer coherence (seconds vs microseconds)
Superconducting qubits have longer coherence
Coherence time is not relevant for QEC

questions.explanation: Trapped ions typically have T2 coherence times of seconds to minutes, compared to microseconds for superconducting qubits. This means fewer QEC cycles are needed to maintain quantum information.

questions.question: What is ion loss and why is it a concern?

options.text: Decoherence of the qubit state
An ion escaping the trap due to collisions, destroying that qubit
Loss of laser power
Reduction in gate fidelity

questions.explanation: Ion loss occurs when background gas molecules collide with a trapped ion, ejecting it from the trap. Though rare (~1/hour), it's catastrophic for that qubit and requires detection and reloading protocols.

questions.question: Why might trapped ions require fewer QEC cycles than superconducting qubits?

options.text: They have higher gate error rates
Their long coherence times allow more gates before errors accumulate
They use better codes
They don't need QEC at all

questions.explanation: With coherence times of seconds and gate times of ~100μs, trapped ions can execute ~100,000 gates before decoherence becomes significant. This reduces the urgency of continuous QEC compared to faster-decohering platforms.

questions.question: Shuttling ions between trap zones in QCCD architecture adds no additional error.
questions.explanation: False. Shuttling adds error through heating of the ion's motion and time spent during which decoherence can occur. Careful trajectory design can minimize but not eliminate these shuttling errors.
